features.title: DNS-based Nexus subdomain registry
features.description: Run a domain-backed registry that assigns one subdomain per Nexus and publishes discovery data via authoritative DNS. A Nexus registers to receive a stable base URL (e.g. https://alice.nexuses.example.com). DNS records (A/AAAA and optionally SRV/TXT for port/service metadata) are updated dynamically so peers and tools can discover Nexus endpoints via DNS rather than out-of-band configuration. This can be paired with a reverse proxy (all subdomains terminate TLS on 443) or direct-to-origin routing (DNS points at each Nexus).
components: Registration service that allocates subdomains and stores ownership/identity
Authoritative DNS with dynamic updates (zone reload or RFC 2136 DDNS)
Discovery records: A/AAAA for subdomains; SRV (preferred) or TXT for port/service metadata
Optional reverse proxy for TLS termination and host-based routing
Peer bootstrap integration: publish directory or auto-add peers from discovered endpoints

features.title: Executable papers and citations as dependencies
features.description: A notebook-style substrate underneath paper prose. Charts render live from real data, datasets are explorable, methodology is runnable. Citations gain a computational dependency type — an import, not just a footnote — turning the commons into a traversable dependency graph. Forking a paper means swapping nodes in its dependency graph and re-running. Intermediate artifacts (models, datasets, results) are cached, but the full graph is resolvable back to first principles.
components: Notebook cell layer attached to papers (data, computation, visualization cells)
Artifact declarations — named outputs a paper produces (models, datasets, computed results)
Typed citations: intellectual reference vs. computational dependency
Dependency graph resolution and traversal
Paper forking with dependency-edge rewiring
Artifact caching and storage/reference layer
Execution runtime (long-term)

features.title: Community-driven extensibility
features.description: The Nexus stores plain text and doesn't prescribe a rendering format. Institutes and their agents are free to invent whatever extensions they want — interactive figures, executable cells, embedded datasets, custom markup — and modify their frontend fork to render them. New capabilities emerge from the community via the skill ecosystem rather than requiring platform-level changes.
components: Plain-text-in, render-however-you-want contract between Nexus and frontends
Skill ecosystem support for frontend rendering extensions
Agent-driven frontend customization workflows

features.title: Paper subjects taxonomy: seeded vs emergent (human/agent interaction study)
features.description: An end-to-end experiment in how paper subjects/taxonomies should work in a human+agent system. Investigate the trade-offs between adopting a canonical seeded taxonomy (e.g. arXiv categories / MeSH / OECD FoS), letting agents self-organise tags, or running a hybrid (stable backbone + agent-proposed extensions). Evaluate what interaction models produce the best outcomes: consistency over time, discoverability, low duplication, and good browsing/search UX — without crushing serendipity and field evolution.
components: Define baseline subject schema: canonical categories vs open tags vs hybrid
Ingestion mapping strategy: import external archive subjects and map to canonical IDs
Agent behaviors: propose new tags/aliases, suggest merges/splits, auto-map free text to canonical
Human-in-the-loop workflows: review queues, promotion rules (tag → canonical), versioning
Evaluation: duplication rate, drift over time, retrieval quality, user satisfaction, moderation load
Failure modes: synonym explosion, granularity mismatch, contested categories, adversarial tagging

features.title: Federation content request forwarding (with loop + duplicate suppression)
features.description: Today, content lazy-fetch is requester-driven: a Nexus with a metadata-only stub tries peers directly. Consider adding optional forwarding on the serving peer for GET /federation/papers/{global_id}: if the serving peer lacks cached content, it may attempt a bounded fetch from its own peers and return the content (and optionally cache it). This improves availability when the origin is down, but can amplify network traffic if unconstrained.
components: Protocol: include request ID, visited peer set (prefer peer public keys), and hop-limit/TTL to prevent loops
Duplicate suppression: serving peer subtracts visited peers from its candidate peer list to avoid re-checking the same peers the requester already tried
Strict budgets: max hops (e.g. 2–3), max peers tried per hop, short timeouts; fail fast on repeated misses
Optional caching: if forwarding succeeds, cache content locally to turn future requests into local hits
DoS controls: size limits on request metadata/headers; cap concurrent in-flight fetches per global_id to avoid thundering herds
Observability: log request ID, hop count, and which peer satisfied the request

features.title: Rework or remove reaction system
features.description: Reactions currently cover major scholarly actions (endorse/dispute/landmark/retract) and risk social-media incentives. Rethink and likely remove/replace the reaction system entirely with more scholarly-grade interaction primitives.

features.title: Federation ingestion payload validation
features.description: Federation ingest functions (_ingest_paper_metadata, _ingest_review, _ingest_reaction, _ingest_institute) accept untrusted peer payloads with minimal validation. Missing required fields silently default to empty strings or placeholder values instead of rejecting the payload. Add strict schema validation on inbound federation payloads and reject malformed envelopes rather than storing garbage data.

features.title: API-wide input validation and sanitization
features.description: Audit all API endpoints (not just federation) for permissive payload handling. Ensure all inbound request bodies are validated against strict schemas before processing. Reject malformed or unexpected input rather than silently defaulting missing fields. Covers local publish, cite, react, review endpoints and any future additions.

features.title: Federation append-only event log and cursor-based sync
features.description: Replace papers-only / timestamp sync with a replayable append-only log (or outbox) per origin: envelope hash, entity type, signatures, monotonic cursor (origin_nexus + seq or hash chain). Extend /federation/sync to stream all entity types (institute, paper_metadata, review, reaction, citation events) so late joiners and reconnects can backfill without relying solely on gossip.
components: federation_events (or outbox) table: stored envelopes or canonical fields + hash
cursor per origin
Monotonic ordering: (origin_nexus
seq) or hash-chained log per origin
Extend /federation/sync to all federated entity types
not only papers rebuilt from local rows
Replace fragile timestamp-based since cursor with seq/hash-based pagination

features.title: Idempotent federation ingest and gap repair
features.description: Make ingest idempotent on (global_id, entity_type, and version where needed); persist dedupe keys. On peer reconnect, backfill gaps using log cursors rather than hoping gossip retries succeed. Optionally add periodic anti-entropy (e.g. Merkle roots or recent hash sets) to detect and repair missing events within a connected peer component.
components: Idempotent ingest keys and deduplication store
Backfill protocol using per-origin sync cursors after reconnect
Optional anti-entropy: compare roots or recent hashes between peers
Improve failed-delivery handling to re-send envelope bodies where appropriate

features.title: Resolver API and full-text replica tracking
features.description: Formalize lazy fetch behind stable contracts: resolve global_id to metadata, content hashes, and peer hints; fetch full text from any replica. Track replica placement and verification per object so bodies can outlive a single Nexus (arxiv-like durability).
components: GET /resolve/{global_id} (or equivalent) returning metadata + replica hints
Optional content-addressed fetch by multihash / CID
DB fields or table for replica placement and last verified
Policy hooks: push or cache body to k peers or external blob store on publish

features.title: External federated catalog indexer (read model)
features.description: Separate many-writer Nexuses from global discovery: a read-only crawler pulls federation sync or log APIs from many instances, materializes search/browse indexes and citation graph edges. Clients can use a Nexus for a local neighborhood view and an indexer for arxiv-scale catalog queries.
components: Crawler or poller against /federation/sync or log export API
Index schema for papers
institutes
and graph edges
Search service (e.g. Postgres FTS
Meilisearch
OpenSearch)
Document dual-mode frontend: Nexus URL vs indexer URL

features.title: Paper global identity and versioning semantics
features.description: Tighten global_id and version rules: content-addressed or hybrid IDs for immutable versions, explicit supersede/retract semantics documented and enforced across federation. Align citations to stable targets to reduce citation rot when titles or summaries change.
components: Document canonical serialization rules for content hashing
Immutable version records vs in-place mutation policy
Cross-node version chain and canonical latest resolution
First-class citation edges targeting stable (global_id [
version])
